message.content:
## Web Searcher: broad/primary — naming inspiration for truth/faithfulness positioning

Research question: "Find the best available brand name for a software product, and verify availability for each finalist. 

THE PRODUCT: an autonomous "deep research" AI agent/pipeline. It fans out web + academic searches across many sources, then writes a long-form research report (e.g. literature reviews, market/industry analyses) in which EVERY claim is traced back to a real, verifiable citation. Its core differentiator vs competitors is FAITHFULNESS and RIGOR — a built-in verifier repairs/re-grounds each sentence to source evidence so the report does not hallucinate. Think of it as "a research analyst that never makes up a fact." Positioning themes to draw from: truth / veracity / faithfulness / proof, depth / synthesis / research, insight / clarity / illumination, and (secondarily) autonomous agentic speed.

CURRENT NAME TO REPLACE: "Polaris" (rejected — too generic, and heavily trademarked: Polaris Inc. snowmobiles, Polaris missiles, many software products). AVOID this and the whole cliché family of star/compass/navigation/"north star" names.

NAMING STYLE: open — prefer short (2-3 syllables), pronounceable, brandable names. Coined/abstract words (like Vercel, Cohere, Anthropic, Kadoa) are welcome because they are the most ownable; obscure real words or Greek/Latin/other-language roots with a truth/insight meaning are also welcome. Avoid the crowded, already-taken names in the AI-research space — explicitly check and EXCLUDE names already used by: Elicit, Consensus, Perplexity, Undermind, Scite, You.com, Genspark, Storm/STORM (Stanford), Ai2/OpenScholar, Scholarcy, Semantic Scholar, Deep Research (OpenAI/Google/Perplexity feature names), Glean, Hebbia, Vespa, and any obvious near-duplicates.

DELIVERABLE: a RANKED shortlist of 8-12 candidate names. For EACH candidate provide:
  1. The name, its pronunciation, and a 1-2 sentence meaning/rationale tying it to the truth/rigor/deep-research positioning.
  2. GitHub availability: is the org/username taken? (check github.com/<name>).
  3. Trademark risk: search USPTO TESS and EUIPO for live marks in the software/AI classes (Nice class 9 and 42); flag any conflict, note if clear.
  4. Domain availability: is <name>.ai and/or <name>.com registered/parked/available? Note approximate acquisition feasibility.
  5. Social handle: is @<name> (or a close variant) free on X/Twitter?
  6. A risk flag: GREEN (all clear), YELLOW (minor/partial conflict), or RED (real conflict) with one-line reason.
Then give a clear TOP RECOMMENDATION (1-2 names) that best balances meaning + full availability across all four channels, and explain why. Prioritize candidates that are GREEN on ALL of: GitHub org, trademark, at least one of .ai/.com, and a social handle. It is better to return fewer names that are genuinely fully-available than many that are taken."

Your angle: **broad/primary — naming inspiration for truth/faithfulness positioning** — Surfaces raw candidate name material and root-word inspiration (verus, sooth, limpid, lucid, evident, proof) that ties directly to the product's faithfulness/rigor/illumination positioning themes.
Search query: `brandable startup name generator truth veracity clarity insight Latin Greek roots two syllable coined word`

## Task
Use WebSearch with the query above (or a refined version). Return the top 4-6 most relevant results.
Rank by relevance to the ORIGINAL question, not just the search query. Skip obvious SEO spam/content farms.
Include a short snippet capturing why each result is relevant.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

I found search results for brandable startup name generators that emphasize clarity, coined words, and brand strategy. Here's what the search reveals:

## Key Findings on Brandable Startup Name Generators:

The tech ecosystem has developed its own naming conventions — short, coined, brandable words that feel modern and signal innovation without trying too hard.

### Name Style Categories:

Several naming approaches are commonly used:

- Brandable Names: Unique, invented words that are easy to remember and trademark (e.g., Google, Looka).
- Compound Names: Blend two relevant words to create a new, meaningful name (e.g., Facebook, FedEx).
- Metaphorical Names: Use powerful imagery to suggest a key benefit or mission (e.g., Amazon for its vast selection).

### Clarity and Branding Strategy:

A simple, clear, and direct name will be far more catchy and easier for customers to recall. If customers don't understand your brand initially, they're less likely to remember it later on.

Regarding coined words specifically, names like Fundavo use a familiar financial cue but turn it into a more brandable coined name that gives enough clarity for the category while keeping it flexible.

### Generator Tools:

Namelix is especially strong at short, brandable outputs. The search results reference several popular generators including Namify, Brandmark, and StartupNameGenerator.ai that focus on producing coined, brandable names with domain and trademark checks.

However, your search included references to Latin/Greek roots and two-syllable requirements—those specific etymological and linguistic features weren't prominently covered in these results.

Based on the search results, here's what AI startups use as naming strategies for coined, brandable names:

## Key Naming Patterns

Successful AI companies use patterns including: Scientific concepts (DeepMind, Stability), Human qualities (Anthropic, Character), Journey metaphors (Runway, Midjourney), Abstract concepts (Cohere, Inflection), and Invented words (Adept, Jasper).

## The "Blank Slate" Strategy

Companies like Notion, Figma, Linear, Vercel, Perplexity, Anthropic, and Mistral don't tell you what the company does. They're distinctive and ownable precisely because they started as blanks.

## Specific Company Reasoning

**Anthropic:** The company chose its name because "the word anthropic means 'relating to humanity,'" and they "wanted to emphasise that we're putting humans at the center of our technology."

**Cohere & Others:** Names like 'Anthropic' or 'Cohere' convey academic credibility, where trust is paramount.

## Psychological Approach

Branding in the AI sector follows clear patterns that combine technical abstraction, functional clarity, and an almost desperate desire to appear human. From names that sound like poetic science like Anthropic or Fathom, to brands with functions embedded in the title, the aim is to condense three things into one word: intelligence, utility, and empathy.

## Practical Considerations

The domain check is critical: whether the .COM or .AI is available. In 2025, having a clean, matching domain is non-negotiable.

The strategy boils down to creating names that are memorable, globally pronounceable, trademark-able, and abstract enough to allow the company to evolve beyond its initial product focus.

Based on my web search results, I have found comprehensive information about the Latin and Old English roots you inquired about. Here's what I discovered:

## Light-Related Roots

The Latin lux, meaning "light," evolved into lucere, "to shine," and expanded into modern languages as words symbolizing brightness and clarity. The roots "luc" and "lum" originate from Latin words for "light" (lux and lumen), emphasizing brightness and clarity in both physical and metaphorical contexts.

The word "lucidus" is related to the Latin verb "lucere," meaning "to shine." This verb goes even further back to the Proto-Indo-European root "*leuk-," which also relates to light and brightness. For example, "lucid" refers to clarity of thought or expression, while "illuminate" can mean to make an idea or concept clear, not just to light up physically.

## Truth-Related Roots

**Verus (Latin):** The Latin root word ver means "truth" or "true." This root is the word origin of a fair number of English vocabulary words, including verdict and veracity. The root "Ver" originates from Latin verus, meaning "true" or "real." Its influence spread through Old French into English during the Norman Conquest, enriching the lexicon with words emphasizing authenticity.

**Sooth (Old English):** Sooth does indeed mean "truth", an Old English word. Sooth 'truth' goes back ultimately to Indo-European *sntyós. This was a derivative of the base *es- 'be', and hence etymologically means 'that which is'. A "soothsayer" came into English early in the fourteenth century, with the meaning one might expect — a person who tells the truth. But within a century it had already been modified to mean somebody who claims to be able to foretell the future.

These roots all share the conceptual link between truth, clarity, and illumination—metaphorically connecting factual correctness with the idea of light revealing what is real and genuine.

content.text: I have strong material now. Let me compile the most relevant results ranked by relevance to the original naming task.

results.title: Word Root: ver (truth) — Membean
results.snippet: Definitive source on the Latin root 'ver' meaning truth/true (verus, veritas, veracity, verify, verdict). This is the single most on-target well of raw name material for the product's faithfulness/veracity positioning — coinable stems like Verus, Verity, Verra, Verid, Sooth-adjacent forms all trace here.

results.title: Veritas — Etymology, Origin & Meaning (Etymonline)
results.snippet: Rigorous etymology of veritas ('truth,' the Roman goddess of truth) from verus. Grounds truth-themed coined names (Verita, Vero, Veritas variants) with a credible academic lineage that fits the 'never makes up a fact' claim. Etymonline is the authoritative etymology reference, not SEO filler.

results.title: Sooth — Etymology, Origin & Meaning (Etymonline)
results.snippet: Documents 'sooth' as Old English for truth, from PIE *sntyós meaning literally 'that which is.' A rare, short, ownable English root directly meaning truth — excellent brandable material (Sooth, Soothe, Sothis) distinct from the crowded Latin field. Note the 'soothsayer=fortune-teller' semantic drift as a risk to weigh.

results.title: Word Root: Luc/Lum (light) — Wordpandit
results.snippet: Covers Latin luc/lum (lux, lumen, lucere 'to shine'; PIE *leuk-) feeding lucid, illuminate, elucidate. Directly serves the illumination/clarity theme — source stems for coined names like Lucid, Lumen, Lucere, Elucid that map onto 'making every claim clear and grounded.'

results.title: Lucid — Etymology, Origin & Meaning (Etymonline)
results.snippet: Traces 'lucid' (clarity of thought/expression) to lucidus/lucere and PIE *leuk-. Reinforces the clarity/illumination angle with authoritative etymology; useful for validating whether a lucid-derived coinage reads as 'clear thinking' vs merely 'bright.'

results.title: Brand Naming in 2026: Why Generated Names Don't Stick — Jacob Tyler
results.snippet: Argues the 'blank slate' strategy behind Notion, Figma, Vercel, Perplexity, Anthropic, Mistral: ownable, distinctive names that don't describe the product. Frames why a coined/abstract root-word name beats a literal star/compass name, and why trademark-clean abstraction matters — directly informs candidate selection strategy over pure generator output.
